analogy: Think of "Liberalisation Privatisation and Globalisation" through the chapter's own opening images: UNIT II ECONOMIC REFORMS SINCE 1991 UNIT III UNIT II After forty years of planned development, India has been able to achieve a strong industrial base and became self-sufficient in the production of food grains. Nevertheless, a major segment of the population continues to depend... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: ECONOMIC REFORMS, SINCE 1991, LIBERALISATION, PRIVATISATION, GLOBALISATION: AN APPRAISAL. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Liberalisation Privatisation and Globalisation is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that uNIT II ECONOMIC REFORMS SINCE 1991 UNIT III UNIT II

The text explains that after forty years of planned development, India has been able to achieve a strong industrial base and became self-sufficient in the production of food grains.

The text explains that nevertheless, a major segment of the population continues to depend on agriculture for its livelihood.

The text explains that in 1991, a crisis in the balance of payments led to the introduction of economic reforms in the country.

The text explains that this unit is an appraisal of the reform process and its implications for India.

The text explains that after studying this chapter, the learners will • understand the background of the reform policies introduced in India in 1991 • understand the mechanism through which reform policies were introduced • comprehend the process of globalisation and its implications for India • be aware of the impact of the reform process in various sectors.

The text explains that lIBERALISATION, PRIVATISATION AND GLOBALISATION: AN APPRAISAL 3

The text explains that 39 LIBERALISATION, PRIVATISATION AND GLOBALISATION: AN APPRAISAL 3.1 INTRODUCTION You have studied in the previous chapter that, since independence, India followed the mixed economy framework by combining the advantages of the capitalist economic system with those of the socialist economic system.

The text explains that some scholars argue that, over the years, this policy resulted in the establishment of a variety of rules and laws, which were aimed at controlling and regulating the economy, ended up instead in hampering the process of growth and development.

The text explains that others state that India, which started its developmental path from near stagnation, has since been able to achieve growth in savings, developed a diversified industrial sector which produces a variety of goods and has experienced sustained expansion of agricultural output which has ensured food security.

The text explains that i n 1 9 9 1 , I n d i a m e t w i t h a n economic crisis relating to its external debt — the government was not able to make repayments on its borrowings from abroad; foreign e x c h a n g e r e s e r v e s , w h i c h w e generally maintain to import petroleum and other important items, dropped to levels that were not sufficient for even a fortnight.

The text explains that the crisis was further compounded by rising prices of essential goods.

The text explains that all these led the government to introduce a new set of policy measures which changed the direction of our developmental strategies.

The text explains that in this chapter, we will look at the background of the crisis, measures that the government has adopted and their impact on various sectors of the economy.

The text explains that 3.2 BACKGROUND The origin of the financial crisis can b e t r a c e d f r o m t h e i n e f f i c i e n t management of the Indian economy in the 1980s.

The text explains that we know that for implementing various policies and its general administration, the government generates funds from various sources such as taxation, running of public sector enterprises etc.

The text explains that when expenditure is more than income, the government borrows to finance the deficit from banks and also from people within the country and from international financial institutions.

The text explains that development policies required that even though the revenues were very low, the government had to overshoot its revenue to meet challenges like unemployment, poverty and population explosion.

The text explains that the continued spending on development programmes of the government did not generate additional revenue.

The text explains that moreover, the government was not able to There is a consensus in the world today that economic development is not all and the GDP is not necessarily a measure of progress of a society.

The text explains that 40 INDIAN ECONOMIC DEVELOPMENT generate sufficiently from internal sources such as taxation.

The text explains that when the government was spending a large share of its income on areas which do not provide immediate returns such as the social sector and defence, there was a need to utilise the rest of its revenue in a highly efficient manner.

The text explains that in the late 1980s, government expenditure began to exceed its revenue by such large margins that meeting the expenditure through borrowings became unsustainable.

The text explains that for availing the loan, these international agencies expected India to liberalise and open up the economy by removing restrictions on the private sector, reduce the role of the government in m a n y a r e a s a n d r e m o v e t r a d e restrictions between India and other countries.

The text explains that the thrust of the policies was towards c r e a t i n g a m o r e c o m p e t i t i v e environment in the economy and removing the barriers to entry and growth of firms.

The text explains that on the other hand, structural reform policies are long-term measures, aimed at improving the efficiency of the economy and increasing its international competitiveness by removing the rigidities in various segments of the Indian economy.

The text explains that the government initiated a variety of policies which fall under three heads

The text explains that deregulation of Industrial Sector: In India, regulatory mechanisms were enforced in various ways (i) industrial licensing under which every entrepreneur had to get permission from government officials to start a firm, close a firm or decide the amount of goods that could be produced (ii) private sector was not allowed in many industries (iii) some goods could be produced only in small-scale industries, and (iv) controls on price fixation and distribution of selected industrial products.

The text explains that the only industries which are now reserved for the public sector a r e a part of a t o m i c e n e r g y generation and some core activities in railway transport.

The text explains that though banks have been given permission to generate resources from India and abroad, certain managerial aspects have been retained with the RBI to safeguard the interests of the account-holders and the nation.

The text explains that tax Reforms: Tax reforms are concerned with the reforms in the government’s taxation and public expenditure policies, which are collectively known as its fiscal policy.

The text explains that there are two types of taxes: direct and indirect.

The text explains that direct taxes consist of taxes on incomes of individuals, as well as, profits of business enterprises.

The text explains that since 1991, there has been a continuous reduction in the taxes on individual incomes as it was felt that high rates of income tax were an important reason for tax evasion.

The text explains that efforts have also been made to reform the indirect taxes, taxes levied on goods and services to facilitate the establishment of a common national market.

The text explains that in the year 2016, Indian constitution was amended to empower state governments and union Government to come out with laws to impose Goods and Services Tax.

The text explains that this is expected to generate additional revenue for the government, reduce tax evasion and create ‘one nation, one tax and one market’.

The text explains that it also set the tone to free the determination of rupee value in the foreign exchange market from government control.

The text explains that trade and Investment Policy Reforms: Liberalisation of trade and investment regime was initiated to increase international competitiveness of industrial production and also foreign investments and technology into the economy.

The text explains that 44 INDIAN ECONOMIC DEVELOPMENT 3.4 P 3.4 P 3.4 P 3.4 P 3.4 PRIV RIV RIV RIV RIVAAAAA TISA TISA TISA TISA TISA TION TION TION TION TION It implies shedding of the ownership or management of a government o w n e d e n t e r p r i s e.

The text explains that g o v e r n m e n t companies are converted into private c o m p a n i e s i n t w o w a y s ( i) b y withdrawal of the government from o w n e r s h i p a n d m a n a g e m e n t o f public sector companies and or (ii) by o u t r i g h t s a l e o f p u b l i c s e c t o r companies.

The text explains that the purpose of the sale, according to the government, was mainly to improve financial discipline and facilitate modernisation.

The text explains that in order to improve efficiency, infuse professionalism and enable them to compete more effectively in the liberalised global environment, the government identifies PSEs and declare them as maharatnas, navratnas and miniratnas.

The text explains that economic Liberalisation, Industrial Structure and Growth in India.

The text explains that india’s Economic Policy: Preparing for the Twenty First Century.

The text explains that oxford University Press, New Delhi, KAPILA, Uma.

The text explains that pAREKH, KIRIT and RADHAKRISHNA, 2002, India Development Report 2001-02.

The text explains that economic Reforms and Poverty Alleviation in India, Sage Publication, Delhi.

The text explains that sACHS, JEFFREY D., ASHUTOSH VARSHNEY and NIRUPAM BAJPAI.1999.

The text explains that government Reports and Websites Economic Survey for various years.

The text explains that published by Oxford University Press, New Delhi.

The text explains that government of India, Planning Commission, New Delhi.

Source: NCERT Class 11 Indian Economic Development, Chapter 3: Liberalisation Privatisation and Globalisation
